source_document: CORRECTED TRANSCRIPT_ Palo Alto Networks Inc.(PANW-US) Q2 2026 Earnings Call 17-February-2026 4_30 PM ET.pdf

exchanges.question_text: Looking back at 2018, 2019, there was a prevailing fear that cloud computing would render parts of the cybersecurity stack obsolete. At that time, you leaned in via M&A and repositioned the portfolio. Now we enter this AI era and the narrative feels oddly similar. Could you compare that existential nature of this AI shift to what we saw in cloud? What areas do you think will be obsolesced? Is M&A the primary lever again this time around, or does your starting position differ?
exchanges.answer_text: In 2018-2019 the challenge was getting customers off on-prem to cloud and delivering services from the cloud, which required refactoring security services and many cloud security acquisitions; XSIAM was architected as cloud-delivered SOC. This time, the market is treating AI as a threat to cybersecurity but customers need consistency in their security stack to respond faster using AI. You cannot respond fast with 70 different vendors with different data, logs, and APIs. We are seeing more consolidation and platformization, evidenced by our best platformization quarter outside Q4. Enterprise AI adoption is slower than consumer, but Prisma AIRS reached 100+ customers faster than cloud security. AI is inevitable and as enterprises put more critical functionality into AI, they will want control, which requires more security.
exchanges.analytical_note: Management frames AI as a tailwind driving platformization and consolidation, with Prisma AIRS adoption outpacing the earlier cloud security ramp.

exchanges.question_text: As we think about XSIAM and the AI-driven SOC, investors are concerned that LLMs are going to kill SIEM tools. How do we think about the balance of opportunity and threat of LLMs doing a lot of the things we relied upon SIEM for? Even if you're competitive from a product standpoint, is there a risk that you face a new strong competitor for these modernization opportunities?
exchanges.answer_text: LLMs are a net positive and additive to security capabilities, useful for data classification, DLP context understanding, and offensive security/red teaming. The challenge is LLMs are 95% right but in security you need to be right 100% of the time against adversaries. Until LLMs reach 99.9% accuracy they are not a threat to delivering security; they are tools that summarize capabilities and enable agentic actions. Every security company will use AI and most have Copilots running in tandem. Our security products sit at edges and create proprietary data and logs that didn't exist before; we are not a system of record but generate domain-specific data, which an LLM cannot replace.
exchanges.analytical_note: Management defends XSIAM's moat by emphasizing proprietary edge-generated data that LLMs cannot replicate, positioning LLMs as complementary rather than competitive.

exchanges.question_text: I'd love to dig into the joint pipeline opportunity with CyberArk. How do you think that opportunity unfolds? And for Dipak, can you help us bridge how much ARR we can include for CyberArk this year, organic versus inorganic?
exchanges.answer_text: CyberArk has a phenomenal field team. Post-close, both teams have been informed on how to pursue joint opportunities, with detailed account planning and a roadmap for overlapping pipeline in the next three to six months. Anecdotally, CyberArk reps have already brought Palo Alto opportunities and BJ Jenkins helped close a CyberArk customer over the weekend, happening in both directions. It is early days and will be crawl-walk-run because systems are different and work is manual, but as teams learn each other's products and we build next-gen products together, momentum will build. Dipak added that they aren't breaking out every M&A deal, but CyberArk NGS ARR was about $1.2 billion as of December 2025 and Chronosphere contributed $200 million ARR, with total M&A contribution guided, so investors have more than breadcrumbs to do the math.
exchanges.analytical_note: Management cites early bidirectional cross-selling anecdotes with CyberArk while acknowledging integration is in early crawl-walk-run phase.

exchanges.question_text: We saw a nice reacceleration in the SASE business in fiscal Q2. Any commentary about what you're seeing driving some of that strength?
exchanges.answer_text: We are excited to see SASE accelerate at scale. First-gen SASE adoption was point-product oriented solving one problem well, but now both new customers and prior customers are returning for a more comprehensive solve because employees work in-office, from home, and while traveling and cannot have three different experiences. Delivering as a platform bridges hardware, software, SASE, and Prisma browser in a consistent way for both security outcomes and end-user productivity. That is the overarching trend driving the SASE business and customer excitement.
exchanges.analytical_note: SASE reacceleration is being driven by displacement of first-generation point products in favor of an integrated platform approach.

exchanges.question_text: What are you seeing in regards to the volume of network traffic from your customers as they move out of experimentation and start to adopt agents enterprise wide? How will that impact demand for the broader network security suite, firewall or SASE?
exchanges.answer_text: It is too early to tell. AI adoption in the enterprise is surging in coding (Codex, Cursor, Claude code), which is endpoint-intensive not infrastructure-intensive, and fits where Koi operates with MCP servers and clients spun up on edges that traditional XDR doesn't see. Outside of that, some customers are running millions of tokens with LLM providers, but that traffic is within the network and networks can handle it. The challenge is consolidating AI traffic in one place for visibility and control. Network traffic is not materially impacted yet, but with $600 billion of data centers being built, traffic will grow; consumer adoption is outstripping enterprise, but enterprise will follow.
exchanges.analytical_note: Management acknowledges AI-driven network traffic growth has not yet materialized in enterprise but expects it to follow consumer adoption as data center buildout completes.

exchanges.question_text: If AI is going to be good for security, when are we going to see it? It doesn't show up in your numbers yet, doesn't show up in anybody's numbers really.
exchanges.answer_text: The best analogy is cloud security where you didn't see numbers for a while because enterprise cloud adoption lagged consumer, then took a two-to-three year cycle for workloads to move. Right now there are few enterprise AI apps driving huge throughput beyond coding apps which are endpoint-intensive. Prisma AIRS customer count is following the same trajectory as XSIAM but volume isn't there because throughput isn't through LLMs yet. You have to believe the $600 billion in data centers will be consumed; we are laying groundwork in an arms race to build the AI security platform. You see protect.ai integrated, the AI firewall, and now Koi being added. The next step is consolidating AI traffic in one place. Be patient.
exchanges.analytical_note: Management acknowledges AI security revenue contribution remains modest and asks for patience, drawing parallels to the multi-year ramp of cloud security.

exchanges.question_text: If CyberArk has historically been strong for privileged users at the high-end, what is the technical lift to make that technology more accessible for every user? And what have you learned in the last six months on the method to securing agentic identity between PAM, IGA and IAM?
exchanges.answer_text: Privileged access management has been a sophisticated category with security-conscious enterprises as biggest adopters, and there is already a transformation to modern PAM with just-in-time controls and zero standing privileges that improves both security and ease of use. We have ideas to integrate CyberArk with Prisma browser to make it easier in the place where users work, driving broader adoption across existing customers and non-customers, leading to a full human identity solution. On agentic identity, it will combine aspects of machine identity and privileged users, which is why CyberArk is well-suited given leadership in both foundational spaces. Some will be standalone CyberArk and some will be in concert with Prisma AIRS, which already has hooks into AI infrastructure.
exchanges.analytical_note: Management positions CyberArk plus Prisma browser/AIRS integration as the path to expand identity security from privileged users to a full human and agentic identity platform.

exchanges.question_text: On Chronosphere's nine-figure expansion with the leading AI provider, what are the key attributes that helped Chronosphere get that level of commitment, displacing an existing vendor, the timing, rationale, and pipeline beyond that? And for Dipak, investors are stripping out $1.47 billion M&A from Q3 NGS ARR and seeing organic net new ARR down a lot—can you clear the air?
exchanges.answer_text: Chronosphere is highly scalable due to a net-new architecture for observability that delivers capabilities at roughly half the price of incumbents, displacing another vendor. They partnered with the LLM provider over six months passing every technical hurdle, with full transition expected over the next 6-12 months. Part of the $200 million ARR is from one of those large LLM vendors and is expected to grow, with other significant customers in pursuit. Lee added Chronosphere built something unique for the very high-end with scalability, economics and early AI analytics that will complement AgentiX; the next phase is building enterprise off-the-shelf features to replace commercial or open source incumbents and scale down into large enterprise. Dipak clarified organic NGS ARR is roughly in line with consensus for Q3 and they reiterated the full year; folks may not have fully appreciated Chronosphere closed before Q2.
exchanges.analytical_note: Management asserts organic NGS ARR guidance is in line with consensus and frames Chronosphere as a structural cost-advantaged disruptor with major LLM provider validation.

exchanges.question_text: On the quantum opportunity, love to hear more about early learnings from discussions with customers at the panel a few weeks back and how you're thinking about the opportunity in coming years.
exchanges.answer_text: As part of the CyberArk deal we acquired Venafi and are building a Next Generation Trust subscription plus our quantum capability. We have been in discussion with 100 customers experimenting with our beta product with tremendous feedback. The quantum capability is not just for Palo Alto firewalls but covers enterprise capability, integrating data from ten other vendors. Lee added that for both cryptography/PQC and certificate management the alternative is manual repetitive work, so they use next-gen firewalls and other data sources to discover everything needed and automate remediation, providing security, reliability and uptime benefits.
exchanges.analytical_note: Management is leveraging the CyberArk/Venafi acquisition to build a Next Generation Trust subscription combining post-quantum cryptography and certificate lifecycle automation with 100 beta customers.

exchanges.question_text: Closing Palo Alto's two largest ever acquisitions within weeks of each other adds unprecedented stress on management, engineering, and go-to-market teams. How do you keep everyone's eye on the ball and avoid execution or distraction issues?
exchanges.answer_text: Chronosphere has been in the works two to three months and CyberArk seven months, with visits to their Boston facility and Israel. We worked with management to understand every employee's role so on close date every employee was informed of their role, OKRs and targets within 48 hours. System transitions are in progress. Chronosphere is a 250-person engineering team in observability which is fundamentally different; the only product interaction is the Cortex team incorporating AgentiX. They are whale hunters going after big observability clients and we help selectively on a client-by-client basis. This is our 32nd or 33rd acquisition, we have lessons from prior deals and have been adding capacity to handle the transitions.
exchanges.analytical_note: Management defends integration readiness citing months of preparation and prior M&A experience, with limited initial product overlap minimizing engineering complexity.
